speaker: interviewer
text: Tape 6 of the interview with Dr. Fritz Gluckstein. You were describing the Russian liberators--

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: --as they came into Berlin. Please continue. Could you tell me how they treated the Berlin populists?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would the Ru-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] quite decently. They saw to it that there was enough food. They brought it in. They actually, for instance, they went to the cemeteries for instance, Jewish cemetery, too, and said, well, you're going to stop growing flowers. The few greenhouses that are still-- some actually survived the bombing, strangely enough-- you are going to, for instance, have tomato plants for the population. And instead of flowers, you grew beans or something else. And actually we went to a cemetery and picked out the tomato plants, See at-- shortly after the war, we moved out to a, a house, actually, in the suburbs. It was quite difficult because of [INAUDIBLE]. And there we had the opportunity to grow our own-- we grow-- we had potatoes, and green beans, and tomatoes. It was quite important. It was one of the reasons why we actually moved out there. If you stayed in the city, it was quite difficult. Even there in the parks, they grew vegetables. And the Russians encouraged it. But right after the war, of course, we were looking for food. And when something else, we said, just-- there are certain neighbors, certain people who harassed us, who are watching whether we really bought our food between 4:00 and 5:00, and whether they would starve us, right. And just wait, what you're going to do to them when the war is over. Now when the war was over, we didn't lower ourselves to go after them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you witness any acts of retribution or revenge on the part of--

speaker: interviewee
text: No I didn't. I didn't. I, I, I was actually always lucky. I was never beaten up. And I actually did not see any physical abuse. Yes, vocal abuse, but I didn't see anything. It has happened. I just was fortunate that it didn't happen to me. And after the war, of course, I was in dreadful shape. I remember the first day I go out. Lo and behold, there-- there were dead men. And there were-- and then I realized, of course, a dead soldier, and another one there. And then there was farther down-- it was quite gruesome-- there was a dead man in the middle of a road. The tank went over him. And that happened after the war too, actually. You tried to get some food. And very soon after the Russians came the Joint Distribution Committee came in with some food. And we in the-- went to the collection point. It was one of the synagogues that survived, and got the food. Momentarily, there was quiet. What happened, they didn't want to give any food rations to my mother. It was quite difficulty. Unfortunately some of the people there in the distribution line [SIGHS] were so anti-German, that any German, even the non-Jewish partners of a mixed marriage-- that-- exactly two hours, it was immediately changed and therefore, we were quiet, of course, everyone.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now these bodies that you were talking about, those were bodies of soldiers who had died in the battle?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And nobody was doing anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how was the health-- how was your health?

speaker: interviewee
text: Reasonably good.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your parents after the hardships?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was fortunate, I never really, thanks to my aunt, really-- yes, I didn't-- I lost some weight. I was okay, but-- I wasn't actually starving, I don't think so. Hungry? Yes. Very hungry, yes. But not starving.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, I'm curious. Who showed up at the-- to the Joint Distribution Committee food lines? Were these people who had survived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Jews who had survived in hiding?

speaker: interviewee
text: Any-- yeah. If you could prove that you were a Jew, you had your identification papers, all-- the answer was yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, do you have a sense of how many Jews did actually survive the war within Berlin.

speaker: interviewee
text: I have no number, no numbers. I have no numbers. No I don't.

speaker: interviewer
text: Something I neglected to ask you before, could you see the ranks of Berlin's Jewish population thinning over time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Once the war started?

speaker: interviewee
text: Look, for instance, in the-- in school, you see. In 1942, the schools were in session. What happened? It was regular school. But one by one, people disappeared. And you knew what happened. Sometimes you went to their home. The apartment was sealed. Sometimes you realized they were picked up on the street. And it was-- for some reason, got used to it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And now was the reverse happening? Were Jews starting to straggle in from camps or from other locations?

speaker: interviewee
text: One-- yes. The ones that had-- were in hiding. And what happened afterwards is some Jews came from east-- Eastern part of Europe, Poland and so on. Of course, they had survived. And the-- they didn't want to stay in Poland. And they had some [INAUDIBLE] camps in Berlin. And there was immediately complaints by some German population. They are black marketeers. Black marketeers, the Americans. At that time the Americans had arrived. Like I say, complained to the American general, who looked closely at the East Germany to the black marketeers. And they got quite an answer. He said, marketeers, buying and selling. If you wouldn't buy, there wouldn't be any black market. As long as you buy, there's a black market. Don't complain. If you don't want a black market, you stop buying and the black market will disappear. That was the end of it. Actually, it was quite interesting after the war, the Russians, Americans, British, and French divided up Berlin. But each, I think, goes by month. One month the Russians were responsible to feed the population, then the Americans, and the British. And the month the Americans were responsible, only white bread. It was quite something. It was unusual. I remember it distinctly.

speaker: interviewer
text: And at that time, it was a very hard, very hard winter. Actually, everything froze. Imagine the pipes froze completely. The-- no water. The toilet facilities were very-- and what you saw, [LAUGHS] very delicate. But what are people to do? They couldn't use their toilet. So you could see the women very elegantly dressed in their fur coats, with little packages to dispose the waste somewhere in the parks. But that--

speaker: interviewee
text: And I tell you, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the potatoes you got, they froze right in your apartment. I remember in that house we lived, we had one little stove, and a little pantry there to keep warm. I went to bed in my overcoat. It was-- but we survived. And then actually I went back to school, special serv-- courses were for Jewish students who had missed school. And some students had taken the final examination as a [NON-ENGLISH] which is necessary, or still is necessary for entrance in the university. And some had taken it after a certain date, I believe after '43. Anything taken after '43 was no longer valid. And they took courses too. Lo and behold, on one side you had the people-- some actually had served in the army with Germans, the other side Jews. Well, we realized-- the Jews realized that not all Germans are really Nazis. And the Germans realized the Jews are actually quite different from what we are led to believe, you see.

speaker: interviewer
text: There were no tensions between Jews and Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Not that. Actually the school was right across from the building where I had an accident. One of the-- I remember one day, fortunate again, a wall collapsed. And it came down on me. And I was standing very closely to the wall collapsed on top. And see, if the stones usually fall-- don't come down the wall. I was standing there. I was knocked out. I had an accident. I still have a scar here. And my vanity-- at the time, my hair-- no hair grows there. But enough hair covered it up. Now you can see it. And there, too, it was fortunate they got me. There was no ambulance. But they got me to a hospital close by, a Catholic hospital. Well, actually the hospital was not supposed to do anything. It was supposed to send me to Jewish hospital. They had me, I saw some younger man and sister. They treated me like any other, sewed me up, and did their very best. And they knew what they did. They broke the rules. They could have gotten into trouble. But they still did it.

speaker: interviewer
text: With whom would they have gotten into trouble?

speaker: interviewee
text: With the authorities.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, this is still during the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: The school, because it just happened to be across where I had that accident, that brings to mind the accident.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, I see. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And right on the same street, lo and behold, I [INAUDIBLE] through, and that happened to me. And the point is you found decent people. They actually took me there. I don't know how. I couldn't walk. They kind of carried me there. And they-- I still remember it-- they took me to a Catholic hospital. And no hospital was allowed to treat any Jews. And me-- but they did it anyway.

speaker: interviewer
text: To what do you attribute the fact that you survived those war years in Berlin?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I say good luck. My mother wasn't Jewish. And I was good luck. It was good luck. Nothing happened to me when I was stopped without a star. Nothing happened to me. I survived the [NON-ENGLISH] Nothing happened to me. When I was standing in the doorway, I didn't get it. The woman next to me. Very fortunate. And, and the accident, I don't know what could have happened. But I survived there. You have to have always some good luck [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Now that the war was over, did you have plans to remain in Berlin?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. I wanted to go, to leave. My father said, if I were 10 years younger, I would go with you. But as a judge, the law is based on old English law in the United States. Here it's Roman law. You go. He also told me, I can't order you. But I urge you, do not ever take a profession that is limited to one country. Look what happened to me. As a lawyer, I am limited. As a Jew you shouldn't do it. He said, you should take a-- you can move from one country to another. Actually what we did after the war--

speaker: interviewer
text: He didn't try to encourage you to remain also in Berlin?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. He felt you should go. Rebuilding, I felt it was not my duty. I  didn't consider it my duty to rebuild Germany. After the war actually, we went to the Friday night, the chaplain center. And there, actually, the chaplain was a Rabbi [INAUDIBLE], Friedman to rabbis. And after the service, it was on a Shabbat, the first time we got to taste gefilte fish. It was great, the first time.

speaker: interviewer
text: There was obviously a letter directed to the chaplain in Berlin. And people asked, please find so-in-so, last address is-- and he said out here, anybody know the people, or anybody know anything about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: And [INAUDIBLE]. And I said, look, my school is close by. Give me the letter. I'm going to check whether they're still there. Some people checked [INAUDIBLE]. Sometimes they were still there. Sometimes the house wasn't standing. House was standing-- or anyhow. And one time, our named was called twice, once from good friends who was in [INAUDIBLE]. And then from the Ginzbergs. The Ginzberg was Louis Ginzberg. He is a Talmud scholar. His wife was a sister of my father's best friend. And lo and behold, we were there. And they got the address, and they got-- a write back. And at that time we realized that we want to go to the United States. I didn't want to stay there. I-- let's go. People-- in fact, it was a colleague of my father's. It was a judge who went, escaped, never took out for his papers. He just stayed there. We found out he had some attachment here. When he came back, he married-- it was, I guess, a friend he married right afterwards, but couldn't. But he just was waiting for the day he could come back to Germany. He said, I did it. You go. You will thank God that you did it. Don't stay here. And but I still waited. I waited at the-- to finish the course at that special-- that school, even took the [INAUDIBLE]. I remember some care packages that arrived before. Those were the care-- the army packages, I guess, C-rations, rations for six men for one day. We loved it. And then of course came, whenever you've got a package, then came the great moment. Each package had cigarettes. But it depends-- it was very important what brand. Because at that time you didn't pay in mark, you paid in cigarettes. And Camel, it was the most valuable. The order was Camel, Chesterfield, Lucky Strike, Phillip Morris, Pall Mall. The last was Raleigh. We were happy if it was Raleigh, but if you got a Camel, ah. It was somewhat difficult, too. My father was back in office.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was reinstated?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. But now we are going and-- go to the black market and pay cigarettes. That wasn't legal. My father never-- he suspected. But of course, you'll never admit it, that we have occasionally. I remember there was one brand which he kept for himself. It was Phillip Morris he liked.

speaker: interviewer
text: He actually smoked those?

speaker: interviewee
text: He still smoked them. Actually my father smoking a cigarette was something to see. He was a cigar smoker. And he held the cigarette like a cigar. And then puff, and looked at it. It was-- but occasionally he took a cigarette.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you did immigrate to the United States. You came alone?

speaker: interviewee
text: I came alone. I remember distinctly, put in for it. I actually got an affidavit, the Joint, and went to the consulate. And I was-- we were interviewed by the vice consul. And for some reason, we talked about that that was-- spent some time in the headquarters of Eichmann.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, wait for a moment. Would you stop, wait outside? 50 minutes later, I come in, and there was some guys, I believe from the Secret Service. They wanted to know. They wanted the layout of the entire headquarters. Tell me, whom did you see? What did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. Very interesting. But I remember distinctly it was so interesting, that Eichmann business. Well, I told him what I knew. I remember they-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there was one antisemitic consul there, [INAUDIBLE], notorious. But thanks goodness, he didn't interview me. I remember the consul, vice consul was Sullivan, very nice. And I got a visa, and he let me go. And I came over here by [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Liberty ship. In the meantime, they rebuilt in some way the Jewish congregation. And people came back, started services again. My father was very active. But I remember what happened. The services started. And there was a young rabbi, actually was a candidate. He survived too. And actually he emigrated to the United States. He gave the first service after the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] liberation. And then one year later, was the first Seder in the courthouse. And they invited all the [INAUDIBLE], the Russians, the French, the British, and the Americans. The Russians didn't come. But they all had a big Seder, the soldiers, and most of the Jewish congregation.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long had it been since you had last had a Seder?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a year. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was-- it couldn't have been '45, because in '45, spring '45, everyone was in shambles. It was '46.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when had been your last Seder prior to 1946?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the Seder was, during the war, we baked our own matzas, [NON-ENGLISH], the bread of affliction. It was really bread of affliction. We were-- What we used? Water and rye flour, alone. That was all we had.

speaker: interviewer
text: It seems like a very authentic kind of matza.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we did that really. It was very hard. Break your teeth on it. But we did it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have a sense of what percentage of the original Berlin Jewish population survived the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: I really don't know at that time. I would say at the end there couldn't have been more than a few thousand. That would include the mixed marriages, and would also include the people that came back of hiding.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what number did you say? I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: A few thousand, I would imagine. But that is just a guess. That is-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] at that time, I really don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now how did you-- where did you come to settle in the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well I came, the Liberty ship. For the first time, I get some food. There was-- at  first they kept us in a kind of camp, close to, it was in Bremen, waiting for Liberty ship to come. And then they sent us on the ship. Very stormy, I remember it. I came over, and never saw the Statue of Liberty, because we arrived there at 2 o'clock in the morning, not Ellis Island. I just remember going on deck and saw the cars driving on Riverside Drive. Yes. And I remember they, they put us on the hotel, the Hotel Marseilles. And there they kept us. You see more or less, what happened at that time, Jews from the East, Shanghai, stayed in the Western part of the United States. And the people coming in from the East, the eastern part, and what happened in the various cities, each family service took so-and-so many refugees. And I came, come the day of interview.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well he talked to me, well, where would you like to go, to Detroit or Saint Paul? Now, I knew enough about the United States, that I'll go to Saint Paul. Lo and behold, they put me on-- it was most interesting in New York for the first time going around. And I was so grateful to my English teachers. At least I could make myself understood.

speaker: interviewee
text: Some people arrived that actually didn't know any English. I think, well, waiting there in Bremen, waiting for the boat to come, they had courses. Some people, ah, we don't bother. We learn in the country. I wonder how they managed coming over here and didn't know one word of English. But probably you always find someone who speak Germany and Yiddish, probably they get along, too. But in New York, it was quite interesting. In the middle of the night, the stores were open. And actually I enjoyed the short stay in New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: They put me on the Trail Blazer on the trip to Saint Paul. I missed the connective train in Saint Paul. The tourists, they said, look. In the afternoon Train 2 takes a number of hours. Why don't you go downtown? You take a bus downtown. Do not run around the neighborhood if you have a nice coat. It's not a very good neighborhood.

speaker: interviewee
text: Go to the bus stop. Take a bus. Go downtown. Come back [INAUDIBLE]. So I did. Took a bus downtown, saw the art gallery down there, and came back. Took the evening Zephyr to Saint Paul. I was met by-- it was William Hoffman. He was a case worker at the St. Paul family service. He took me under his wing. And I was in the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was 21 on the boat. Exactly I came here, the 30th or 31st of January, I arrived in New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the year again?

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do in Saint Paul?

speaker: interviewee
text: Saint Paul, I got to work at first a refrigerator company. Actually, pretty rough work, that glass, fiberglass. But I got used it and there too, they try to teach me ah, we're going to teach him some four-letter words, and embarrass him. And I had to tell them, don't try. The four-letter words are Anglo-Saxon. They are identical or almost identical to the German one. It doesn't work. And then I worked at a hospital as a kind of orderly or porter. And then I finally started-- in October, '48, I started school, University of Minnesota. Had a job in the morning, noon, and night. I worked at the-- as a busboy, at the union. And in the summertime worked as a janitor. And sometimes at night I-- they had earmuffs. The earmuffs had funny faces, if you recall, with earmuff. And outside was funny. I did those. And actually at the Hillel House, I got a room there. I was assistant caretaker.

speaker: interviewer
text: Of the Hillel House?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of the Hillel House, particularly at nights helping out, weekends when the regular caretaker wasn't there, Sundays. And that's school and work.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you get any assistance from HIAS or the Joint?

speaker: interviewee
text: Actually what happened, I got a scholarship to get some money toward tuition from the family service. I forgot the name. It's a Jewish women, association of Jewish women. I get some help there. But more or less to defray the cost for the tuition. It was fortunate, too, I came to Minnesota. And this was very important that I was treated as a-- considered a resident. Because the fees, tuition were much higher. But lo and behold, I had to appear before a board of residency. And they wanted to know why are you here. And I told them, I'm here to live, not to study. Well, you're hereby accepted as a resident. That saved quite a bit.

speaker: interviewer
text: How-- how were you treated by American Jews? Did they ask you what life had been like for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, of course, what happened at the Hillel House, we had a Purim party. And there was-- it had a booth there, what have you-- taking chances and whatever, to-- playing puzzles. And lo and behold, there was a pie-throwing contest. You could buy a pie, and throw it in somebody's face. And I got very upset. They didn't understand why I would get upset. But the rabbi, he explained, look. The food, he wasn't used to it, wasting food. He probably still remembers the time he didn't have anything to eat. And now they throw. But it was quite interesting. It was one time, it was not a Jewish. It was Polish, another refugee. Lo and behold, we talked to the rabbi, and I mentioned to him, the last service I happened to go to a conservative time, the rabbi had to stop the service to ask for quiet. Well, he said, it was Rabbi [? Milgrim ?], and he laughed. Look, he said, and he turned to the young man from Poland. Where you come from, what's going on here is whispering. And he turned to me and said, where you come from, what's going on here is yelling. In Germany, you wouldn't say anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: The decorum was different.

speaker: interviewee
text: The decorum. See, in Poland it didn't matter. Here it was just in between. But in Germany you didn't interrupt the service, for-- at least in the [INAUDIBLE] synagogue. Now it was, actually-- and I met Ethel there. Meet your spouse at the Hillel House. See, actually what happened, it was Sunday night. And the fraternity didn't serve, and they came to the Hillel House to have some food. And I go downstairs, and tell him, now look he is [? Milkik ?] and he is [? Frakik ?]. And don't mix them up. OK. I go upstairs. Five minutes later, I come down. They had them all mixed up. And there was a redhead there said, tell me about it. And I complained about her. That was Ethel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that's how you met your future wife.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to pause now to change the tape.
